bytes.topic: Architecture & Structure â€” file-based routing as architecture, route-level colocation of loader/error/loading
bytes.title: SvelteKit File-Based Routing Is Your Architecture â€” Not Just Your URL Map
bytes.body:
Every directory under `src/routes/` in SvelteKit is a self-contained feature unit, not just a URL segment. The folder declares its own data contract (`+page.server.ts`), error boundary (`+error.svelte`), and layout inheritance (`+layout.svelte`) â€” the filesystem is your architecture.

The `load` function in `+page.server.ts` runs exclusively on the server, returning a typed `PageData` object inferred from `./$types`. Throw SvelteKit's `error()` helper inside `load` and the nearest `+error.svelte` up the route tree catches it automatically â€” no try/catch in the component.

SvelteKit has no `+loading.svelte`. Instead, return an unresolved `Promise` from `load` and use `{#await}` in `+page.svelte` for streamed, progressive rendering â€” the page shell arrives immediately while deferred data resolves.

Route groups like `(auth)/` let you wrap routes in a shared `+layout.svelte` (e.g., auth guard, different shell) without polluting the URL. The group name is invisible to the router.

The result: open any route folder and instantly know what data it fetches, how it fails, and how it renders â€” zero cross-file archaeology required.

bytes.topic: Component Design â€” Server Components vs Client Components, push use client to the leaves
bytes.title: SvelteKit Component Design â€” Keep the Server at the Root, Push Interactivity to the Leaves
bytes.body: SvelteKit doesn't have a `use client` directive, but the same architectural principle applies: load data on the server, hydrate only where interactivity is needed. Your `+page.server.ts` is your server boundary â€” it runs exclusively on the server and its return value flows as props into your component tree. The mistake is reaching for `onMount` or reactive stores at the layout or page level when the data could simply arrive from `load()`. Structure your tree so `+page.server.ts` owns all data fetching, `+page.svelte` is a thin shell that distributes props, and only leaf components â€” a `<LikeButton>`, a `<CommentInput>` â€” wire up event handlers, `writable` stores, or `onMount` for client-only effects. This lets SvelteKit stream and prerender the outer shell while deferring hydration cost to small, focused components. Guard any truly client-only initialization with `if (browser)` from `$app/environment` rather than sprinkling `onMount` everywhere. The result: faster TTI, smaller hydration surface, and a clear mental model of what runs where.

bytes.topic: State Management â€” URL as state for filters/pagination, server state in loaders not useState
bytes.title: SvelteKit URL State â€” Filters and Pagination Belong in the URL, Not $state
bytes.body:
SvelteKit's `load()` functions are the right home for server-derived data â€” not `$state` runes or writable stores. For filters and pagination, encode them in `url.searchParams` and let `load()` react automatically. SvelteKit re-runs any `load()` that reads `url.searchParams` whenever those params change, so `goto()` becomes your state setter â€” no manual invalidation needed.

In `+page.server.ts`, read `url.searchParams` directly and return typed data. The `data` prop in `+page.svelte` updates reactively on every navigation. When a filter changes, call `goto()` with an updated `URL` object. Pass `noScroll: true` and `keepFocus: true` to prevent jarring resets on filter interactions.

Avoid mirroring `data.results` into a `$state` variable â€” that creates a second source of truth and silently breaks re-runs. If you need imperative invalidation (e.g., after a mutation), call `invalidate('app:results')` paired with `depends('app:results')` in `load()`, not a local state copy.

This pattern gives you bookmarkable, shareable URLs, free browser history navigation through filter states, and zero client/server sync bugs â€” all without a single `$state` declaration for server data.

bytes.topic: Performance â€” SSG vs SSR vs ISR decision matrix, parallel data fetching in loaders
bytes.title: SvelteKit SSG vs SSR vs ISR â€” Decision Matrix and Parallel Loader Performance
bytes.body:
SvelteKit's rendering mode is controlled by exports in `+page.ts` or `+page.server.ts`, not config files. Use `export const prerender = true` for SSG â€” correct for docs, marketing, or any route with zero per-request variance. Default SSR runs your `load` on every request; keep it for auth-gated, personalized, or real-time pages. ISR isn't native to SvelteKit's core, but `adapter-vercel` exposes `export const config = { isr: { expiration: 60 } }` per-route for edge revalidation. For self-hosted deploys, set `Cache-Control: stale-while-revalidate` in your `handle` hook instead.

The highest-yield SSR optimization is eliminating waterfall fetches inside `load`. SvelteKit lets you return unawaited promises â€” the runtime streams them to the client, enabling `{#await}` blocks to resolve independently. For server-only parallel fetches, use `Promise.all` over SvelteKit's injected `fetch`, not `globalThis.fetch`. The injected version forwards cookies automatically, deduplicates identical requests within the same render cycle, and resolves relative URLs against the origin â€” none of which global fetch does in a Node SSR context.

bytes.topic: Data Fetching â€” route loaders fetch before render, eliminate waterfall, no fetch inside components
bytes.title: SvelteKit Route Loaders â€” Kill the Waterfall Before the Component Renders
bytes.body: SvelteKit's `load` function in `+page.server.ts` runs on the server before your component mounts â€” data arrives with the HTML, not after. This eliminates the fetch-on-mount waterfall that plagues component-level data fetching. If a page needs a post and its author, fire both requests in parallel inside a single `load` using Promise.all or by returning multiple awaited calls â€” SvelteKit resolves them concurrently. The typed `data` prop lands in `+page.svelte` fully resolved, no loading spinners required for initial paint. Nest `+layout.server.ts` loaders for shared data like auth state; child `load` functions run in parallel with their parent layout loaders automatically. SvelteKit's enhanced `fetch` inside `load` is credential-forwarded, SSR-safe, and deduplicated â€” never reach for `fetch` inside `onMount` or `<script>` blocks for route-critical data. For client-side navigations, loaders re-run transparently via `invalidate()` or `invalidateAll()`, giving you fine-grained cache control without a state library.

bytes.topic: Forms â€” Server Actions, progressive enhancement, form validation on server boundary
bytes.title: SvelteKit Forms â€” Server Actions, Progressive Enhancement, and Server-Side Validation
bytes.body: SvelteKit's form actions run exclusively on the server via the `actions` export in `+page.server.ts`. Your `<form>` POSTs to the same route by default â€” no `fetch`, no API endpoint needed. Validation happens in the action using `request.formData()`, and failures are returned with `fail(422, { errors })`, which SvelteKit sends back as `ActionData`. Bind it in `+page.svelte` via the `form` prop (typed as `ActionData`). This works with zero JavaScript â€” true progressive enhancement. Add `use:enhance` from `$app/forms` to intercept the submission client-side, skip the full-page reload, and still receive the same `ActionData` back. The `enhance` callback gives you fine-grained control: optimistic UI, custom loading states, or preventing the default update entirely. Crucially, `fail()` sets the HTTP status without throwing, so SvelteKit keeps the form data in scope for re-population. Never use `throw redirect()` on validation failure â€” that drops the error payload. Server actions also automatically protect against CSRF via SvelteKit's built-in origin check, so form submissions from foreign origins are rejected without any extra middleware.

bytes.topic: TypeScript â€” typed route params, typed loaders, typed search params with Zod
bytes.title: SvelteKit â€” Typed Route Params, Loaders, and Search Params with Zod
bytes.body:
SvelteKit's generated `$types` module gives you `PageLoad`, `PageServerLoad`, and `RouteParams` â€” but these are structural types, not validated at runtime. Combine them with Zod to get both compile-time safety and runtime validation in a single pass.

In `+page.server.ts`, destructure `params` typed via `PageServerLoad` and parse with a Zod schema. For search params, SvelteKit passes them through `url.searchParams` â€” not typed by default. Zod's `z.coerce` handles the string-to-number conversion that `URLSearchParams` always returns.

The key SvelteKit-specific detail: `load` functions run both on the server and client during SSR hydration. A Zod `safeParse` lets you throw a `redirect` or `error` (from `@sveltejs/kit`) cleanly inside `load` without leaking stack traces to the client. Typed returns from `load` flow into `+page.svelte` via the `data` prop, which `PageData` narrows automatically â€” no casting needed downstream.

bytes.topic: Error Handling â€” error.tsx / +error.svelte per route segment, notFound(), redirect()
bytes.title: SvelteKit Error Handling â€” +error.svelte Boundaries, notFound(), and redirect()
bytes.body:
SvelteKit's error model is route-segment-scoped. Drop a `+error.svelte` next to any `+page.svelte` or `+layout.svelte` and it catches errors thrown within that segment â€” without taking down the surrounding shell. If a segment lacks one, the error bubbles to the nearest ancestor `+error.svelte`, ultimately falling back to `src/routes/+error.svelte`.

Throw structured errors in `load` or form actions using `error(status, message)` from `@sveltejs/kit`. Use `notFound()` as a semantic shorthand for `error(404)`, and `redirect(303, '/path')` for post-auth or post-mutation flows â€” both are `throw`-based, so SvelteKit intercepts them before your `return` is ever evaluated.

Inside `+error.svelte`, `$page.error` exposes the thrown object and `$page.status` gives the HTTP code â€” no prop drilling, no try/catch in the component. The component still inherits parent layout data via `$page.data`, so your nav shell stays intact on error pages.

One footgun: errors thrown outside `load` â€” e.g., in a module-level utility â€” bypass the boundary entirely and surface as unhandled exceptions. Keep all throwing inside `load`, `actions`, or `hooks.server.ts` `handle`.

bytes.topic: Testing â€” test loaders and actions in isolation, Playwright for full route flows
bytes.title: SvelteKit Testing â€” Isolate Loaders & Actions, Then Verify Full Flows with Playwright
bytes.body:
SvelteKit's `load` functions and form actions are pure-ish functions â€” test them directly without spinning up a server. Import your `+page.server.ts` loader and call it with a mock `RequestEvent`. Assert on the returned data shape. For actions, call the named export (e.g. `actions.default`) with a fake `request` containing `FormData`. No `fetch` mocking ceremony needed for most cases â€” just construct the event object.

For full route integration, Playwright's `page.goto('/dashboard')` exercises the actual SvelteKit router, SSR, and hydration. Use `page.waitForSelector` after navigation to assert hydrated state, not just SSR HTML. For authenticated routes, seed cookies via `context.addCookies` before the test â€” Clerk or any JWT-based session can be injected this way.

Combine both layers: unit tests catch broken loader logic fast; Playwright catches broken routing, redirects (`throw redirect(302, '/login')`), and form action side-effects that only surface end-to-end. Skip testing `+layout.server.ts` in isolation unless it contains non-trivial logic â€” the E2E layer covers it implicitly.

bytes.topic: Security â€” Server Actions as trust boundary, validate all input, env var separation
bytes.title: SvelteKit Server Actions â€” Every formData Value Is Hostile Until Proven Otherwise
bytes.body:
SvelteKit's `actions` export in `+page.server.ts` runs server-side only, but network reachability doesn't equal safety â€” every `request.formData()` value arrives as an untyped string from the wire. Validate with Zod before any DB write or downstream call. The `fail()` helper from `@sveltejs/kit` returns structured 4xx responses without throwing, keeping your action's happy path readable.

The env module system is where SvelteKit's trust model really shines. Secrets belong in `$env/static/private` or `$env/dynamic/private` â€” the bundler raises a hard build error if these are imported inside any client-evaluated module (`+page.ts`, `+layout.ts`, a component). Public configuration lives in `$env/static/public`. Never fall back to `process.env` directly: it bypasses SvelteKit's enforcement entirely and its behavior inside client bundles is undefined.

`load` in `+page.server.ts` carries the same obligations as actions. User-controlled inputs like `params.id` or `url.searchParams.get()` are trust-boundary values too â€” parse and narrow them with the same rigor before they touch business logic.

bytes.topic: Code Quality â€” route segment conventions, consistent loader/action naming
bytes.title: SvelteKit Route Conventions â€” Consistent Naming for Loaders and Actions
bytes.body:
SvelteKit's file-based routing gives you `+page.server.ts`, `+layout.server.ts`, and `+server.ts` â€” but code quality degrades fast when teams name exported functions inconsistently. The convention: always name your `load` function `load` and your form actions descriptively, never default.

In `+page.server.ts`, export a named `load` function (typed with `PageServerLoad`) and a named `actions` object with verb-first keys like `create`, `update`, or `deletePost`. This makes `$page.form` errors traceable and `use:enhance` bindings explicit.

Avoid exporting anonymous arrow functions as `load` â€” type inference breaks and stack traces become unreadable. For `+layout.server.ts`, prefix shared data keys with the layout segment name (e.g., `dashboardUser`) to prevent key collisions when layout and page `load` data are merged into `$page.data`.

Use `satisfies` instead of `as` for typing `actions` â€” it preserves the literal action keys so `ActionResult` generics resolve correctly downstream in your `+page.svelte` `ActionData` type.

bytes.topic: Routing â€” file-based routing patterns, dynamic segments, catch-all, intercepting routes, parallel routes
bytes.title: SvelteKit Routing Deep Dive â€” Dynamic Segments, Catch-Alls, and Groups
bytes.body:
SvelteKit's filesystem router maps `src/routes` to URLs with zero config. A folder named `[slug]` creates a dynamic segment â€” accessible via `params.slug` in both `+page.svelte` and `+page.server.ts` load functions. Wrap a segment in `[[optional]]` to make it optional without creating a separate route.

Catch-all routes use `[...rest]` â€” `src/routes/docs/[...path]` matches `/docs/a/b/c`, giving you `params.path = 'a/b/c'`. Combine with a typed `ParamMatcher` in `src/params/` to validate segments before the route resolves.

Route groups `(group)` organize routes without affecting the URL â€” perfect for sharing a layout between `/dashboard` and `/settings` without a common path prefix. Parallel routes don't exist natively in SvelteKit; the idiomatic substitute is named layout slots via `@` resets â€” e.g., `+page@` breaks out of a specific ancestor layout. For intercepted routes (modal patterns), use shallow routing: `goto(url, { state: { modal: true } })` and read `$page.state` to conditionally render a modal overlay instead of navigating away.

bytes.topic: Internationalization â€” i18n routing strategies, locale in URL vs cookie, server-side locale detection
bytes.title: SvelteKit i18n â€” URL Locale vs Cookie, and Server-Side Detection
bytes.body:
SvelteKit gives you two primary locale strategies: encode it in the URL (`/en/about`, `/fr/about`) or store it in a cookie. URL-based locale is SEO-friendly and stateless â€” ideal for public content. Cookie-based is cleaner for authenticated apps where locale is a user preference.

For URL routing, use a `[lang]` param segment and a `+layout.server.ts` to load translations. Read `params.lang` in `load()` and pass it down via `$page.data`.

For server-side detection without a URL param, use the `handle` hook in `hooks.server.ts`. Parse `event.request.headers.get('accept-language')` to extract the preferred locale, then either redirect to the localized route or set a `locale` cookie via `event.cookies.set()`. Always prefer `event.locals` to propagate the resolved locale to all `load()` functions downstream â€” avoids redundant header parsing per route.

Avoid mixing strategies: if your `load()` reads from both `params.lang` and a cookie, you'll get hydration mismatches on the client.

bytes.topic: Web Vitals â€” image optimization with framework Image component, font loading, LCP via priority flag
bytes.title: SvelteKit Web Vitals â€” Image Optimization, Font Loading, and LCP Tuning
bytes.body:
SvelteKit doesn't ship a built-in `<Image>` component like Next.js, but `@sveltejs/enhanced-img` fills that gap. Add it via `vite-imagetools` integration and use `<enhanced:img>` in your markup â€” it automatically generates WebP/AVIF variants, injects width/height to prevent layout shift, and produces a `srcset` without manual configuration.

For LCP images, pass `fetchpriority="high"` and skip `loading="lazy"` â€” the browser needs that resource immediately. Pairing this with a `<link rel="preload">` in your `<svelte:head>` block gives the earliest possible fetch signal.

Font loading is handled cleanly in SvelteKit's `app.html` or via a layout's `<svelte:head>`. Use `font-display: optional` for body fonts to eliminate render-blocking; for brand fonts critical to LCP, `font-display: swap` with a preconnect to your font CDN is the safer tradeoff.

Measure with `$app/environment`'s `browser` guard to run `PerformanceObserver` only client-side, capturing real LCP entries without SSR noise.
bytes.code_snippet:
<!-- src/routes/+layout.svelte -->
<svelte:head>
  <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />
  <link
    rel="preload"
    as="image"
    href="/hero.avif"
    fetchpriority="high"
  />
</svelte:head>

<!-- Hero component -->
<script>
  import { browser } from '$app/environment';
  import { onMount } from 'svelte';

  onMount(() => {
    if (!browser) return;
    const observer = new PerformanceObserver((list) => {
      for (const entry of list.getEntries()) {
        console.log('LCP candidate:', entry.startTime, entry.element);
      }
    });
    observer.observe({ type: 'largest-contentful-paint', buffered: true });
  });
</script>

<enhanced:img
  src="/hero.png"
  alt="Hero"
  fetchpriority="high"
  loading="eager"
/>

bytes.topic: Memory Leaks â€” streaming with Suspense, abort signals in loaders, cleanup on navigation
bytes.title: SvelteKit Loaders Leak Without Abort Signals â€” Here's the Fix
bytes.body:
SvelteKit's `load` functions receive a `signal` â€” an `AbortSignal` that fires when the client navigates away or the HTTP connection closes. SvelteKit's built-in `fetch` wrapper honours it automatically, but any native `fetch`, `EventSource`, or `ReadableStream` you open yourself does not.

When you return an unresolved promise from a server `load` (deferred streaming), SvelteKit pipes chunks to the client over an open HTTP connection. If your async generator never checks `signal.aborted`, it keeps producing chunks server-side long after the user has moved on â€” leaking both memory and open connections.

On the client, `onDestroy` is too late for in-flight requests started after mount. Use `beforeNavigate` instead: it fires before the outgoing route tears down, giving you a clean window to `abort()` any active controllers. `afterNavigate` resets the controller for back-navigation into the same page. Pair this with `onDestroy` as a final safety net for tab-close scenarios.

The pattern: one `AbortController` per page instance, aborted in `beforeNavigate`, reset in `afterNavigate`, and passed as `signal` to every outbound `fetch`.
bytes.code_snippet:
<!-- +page.svelte -->
<script lang="ts">
  import { beforeNavigate, afterNavigate } from '$app/navigation';
  import { onDestroy } from 'svelte';

  let controller = new AbortController();

  // beforeNavigate fires before unmount â€” ideal for aborting in-flight work
  beforeNavigate(() => controller.abort());

  // Reset after back-navigation so the page is usable again
  afterNavigate(() => { controller = new AbortController(); });

  // Final safety net for tab close / component destroy
  onDestroy(() => controller.abort());

  async function loadMore() {
    const res = await fetch('/api/feed/next', { signal: controller.signal });
    if (!res.ok || controller.signal.aborted) return;
    // handle chunks
  }
</script>

bytes.topic: Real-time & WebSockets â€” server-sent events from route handlers, combining with client state
bytes.title: SvelteKit SSE â€” Streaming Server-Sent Events from Route Handlers into Svelte Stores
bytes.body:
SvelteKit's `+server.ts` route handlers can stream SSE by returning a `Response` wrapping a `ReadableStream` with `Content-Type: text/event-stream`. The `start` callback receives a `ReadableStreamDefaultController` â€” enqueue raw SSE-formatted strings (`data: ...

`) directly. Return a cancel function from `start` to clean up timers or DB subscriptions when the client disconnects.

On the client, use the browser's native `EventSource` API pointed at your route, then pipe incoming messages into a Svelte `writable` store. Wrap the connection in `onMount` and return `source.close` as the cleanup function â€” Svelte's lifecycle handles disconnection on component destroy automatically.

For server-side load data coexisting with the stream, keep concerns separated: use `+page.server.ts` for initial hydration and SSE for deltas only. This avoids race conditions where the initial `load` and the first SSE event overwrite each other. If you need auth, validate the JWT inside the `GET` handler via `request.headers.get('cookie')` before opening the stream â€” `EventSource` sends cookies but ignores `credentials: 'include'` since it predates Fetch.

bytes.topic: Environment & Config â€” NEXT_PUBLIC_ vs private vars, validate at startup, never expose secrets
bytes.title: SvelteKit Environment Variables â€” PUBLIC_ Boundaries, Startup Validation, and Secret Safety
bytes.body:
SvelteKit splits env vars into two enforced namespaces. Variables prefixed with `PUBLIC_` are accessible from `$env/static/public` or `$env/dynamic/public` and can be imported in components, pages, and client-side `+page.ts` files. Everything else lives behind `$env/static/private` or `$env/dynamic/private` â€” importable only in server-side files like `+page.server.ts`, `+server.ts`, and `hooks.server.ts`. This isn't a convention â€” SvelteKit's module graph analysis throws a build error if you import a private env module from a shared or client-accessible file.

Validate required vars at startup inside `hooks.server.ts`. It runs once on server boot, surfaces misconfigurations in CI before requests are served, and avoids null-reference crashes buried inside request handlers. Prefer `$env/dynamic/private` over `$env/static/private` when you need runtime flexibility (e.g. container deployments). Never reach for `process.env` directly â€” SvelteKit's env modules are tree-shaken, statically typed, and enforce the public/private boundary at the compiler level.

bytes.topic: API Layer â€” route handlers for public APIs, Server Actions for mutations, middleware for auth
bytes.title: SvelteKit API Layer â€” Route Handlers, Server Actions, and Hooks for Auth
bytes.body:
SvelteKit gives you three distinct tools for server-side logic, and conflating them causes pain.

**`+server.ts` route handlers** are for public API endpoints â€” think REST or webhook receivers. They export named HTTP method functions (`GET`, `POST`, etc.) and return a `Response`. Use these when external clients need to hit your API.

**Server Actions** (form `actions` in `+page.server.ts`) are for mutations triggered from your own UI. They run on `POST`, integrate with `enhance`, and return typed data back to the page via `ActionData`. No `fetch` needed â€” SvelteKit wires it automatically.

**`hooks.server.ts`** is where auth lives. The `handle` hook intercepts every request before it reaches a route. Set `event.locals.user` here after validating your session cookie or JWT, then read `locals.user` in any `load` function or action downstream â€” one validation point, zero repetition.

The golden rule: never do auth inside individual `load` functions. Centralize it in `handle`, guard routes by checking `locals` early, and redirect in the hook itself using `error(401)` or `redirect(303)`.

bytes.topic: Animation â€” page transition patterns, View Transitions API, layout persistence across navigations
bytes.title: SvelteKit Page Transitions â€” View Transitions API + Layout Persistence
bytes.body:
SvelteKit's `onNavigate` lifecycle hook is the native integration point for the View Transitions API. Call `document.startViewTransition()` inside it and return a promise â€” SvelteKit holds the navigation until your promise resolves, giving the browser time to capture the old and new DOM states.

For layout persistence across navigations, the key is understanding that `+layout.svelte` components are never unmounted when child routes change â€” only the `<slot />` region re-renders. This means elements like sidebars and navbars animate naturally. To persist a specific element's visual identity across routes (e.g., a shared card expanding into a detail view), assign matching `view-transition-name` CSS properties on both pages.

CSS `::view-transition-old` and `::view-transition-new` pseudo-elements let you override the default cross-fade per named element. Pair this with SvelteKit's `preloadData` on hover to ensure data is ready before the transition fires, preventing a flash of loading state mid-animation. For fallback, wrap in `if (document.startViewTransition)` â€” `onNavigate` still fires in unsupported browsers.

bytes.topic: Global UI Patterns â€” layout-level toast/modal, shared loading UI in layout segment
bytes.title: SvelteKit Layout-Level Toast, Modal & Shared Loading UI â€” Global UI Patterns Done Right
bytes.body:
SvelteKit's nested layout system makes it trivial to wire up global UI without prop-drilling or a state management library. Drop a `+layout.svelte` at the root and use a writable store to drive toasts and modals from anywhere in the app.

Define a `toastStore` and `modalStore` in `$lib/stores/ui.ts`. Your root layout subscribes to both and renders the overlay components once â€” they're always mounted, always reactive. Child routes trigger them by importing the store and calling `toastStore.show(...)` or `modalStore.open(...)`.

For shared loading UI, SvelteKit exposes the `$navigating` store from `$app/stores`. It's non-null during any client-side navigation. Bind a progress bar or skeleton overlay directly to `$navigating` in `+layout.svelte` â€” no lifecycle hooks needed. Pair it with `+loading.svelte` (introduced via route-level `+page.svelte` async `load`) for streamed data skeletons scoped to a layout segment. Use `+layout.ts` with `await parent()` to sequence data dependencies, keeping the root layout's loading state decoupled from nested route fetches.
bytes.code_snippet:
<!-- src/routes/+layout.svelte -->
<script lang="ts">
  import { navigating } from '$app/stores';
  import { toastStore } from '$lib/stores/ui';
  import Toast from '$lib/components/Toast.svelte';
  import Modal from '$lib/components/Modal.svelte';
</script>

{#if $navigating}
  <div class="progress-bar" />
{/if}

<Toast messages={$toastStore.messages} />
<Modal config={$modalStore} />

<slot />

bytes.topic: Bundle & Asset Optimization â€” automatic code splitting per route, dynamic imports, bundle analysis
bytes.title: SvelteKit's Automatic Code Splitting â€” Route Chunks, Dynamic Imports, and Bundle Analysis
bytes.body:
SvelteKit splits your bundle automatically at route boundaries. Every `+page.svelte`, `+layout.svelte`, and `+page.server.ts` becomes its own chunk â€” Vite handles this without configuration. The client only downloads JavaScript for the routes it actually visits.

For heavy components inside a route, reach for dynamic imports via `{#await import(...)}` or wrap with `svelte:component` after a lazy load. This creates a secondary async chunk, keeping the initial route payload lean.

Server-side modules (`+page.server.ts`, `+server.ts`) are never bundled into the client chunk at all â€” SvelteKit's module graph analysis strips them entirely, so you get zero leakage of server-only code.

To inspect what ends up in each chunk, run `vite build --mode production` then open `.svelte-kit/output/client/_app/immutable/` â€” the filenames include content hashes and you can cross-reference with `vite-bundle-visualizer`:

```
npx vite-bundle-visualizer
```

For granular control, use `$app/environment`'s `browser` guard combined with dynamic import to defer third-party libraries (e.g. chart renderers) until after hydration completes, preventing them from blocking the critical path.
bytes.code_snippet:
{#if ChartComponent} {:else}

Loading chart...

{/if}

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling & Quality Gates â€” framework CLI, preview deployments, type-check route params
bytes.title: SvelteKit Quality Gates â€” Type-Safe Route Params, CLI Sync, and Adapter-Aware Previews
bytes.body:
SvelteKit's CLI ships a `sync` command that generates `.svelte-kit/types/` â€” the source of the `./$types` virtual module every route imports. Run `svelte-kit sync` before `svelte-check` in CI, or let both fire automatically via `npm run check` (scaffolded by `create-svelte` by default).

Route params get strict types through `./$types`: import `PageServerLoad` or `PageLoad` in your `+page.server.ts` / `+page.ts`, and `params` becomes typed against your file-system segments automatically. A route at `src/routes/posts/[slug]/+page.server.ts` surfaces `params.slug: string` â€” no manual casting. For optional or rest segments (`[[optional]]`, `[...rest]`), the generated `RouteParams` type reflects correct optionality.

For preview deployments, `vite preview` serves the Vite-built output locally but skips adapter-specific runtime behavior. For Cloudflare, use `wrangler pages dev .svelte-kit/cloudflare`; for Vercel, use `vercel dev`. These surface edge cases â€” missing platform bindings, broken `PLATFORM` env vars â€” that `vite preview` silently swallows.

Gate your CI pipeline with `svelte-kit sync && svelte-check --tsconfig ./tsconfig.json` to catch broken param types and `.svelte` type errors before any branch merges.

bytes.topic: Monitoring in Production â€” edge function logs, server-side Sentry, Web Vitals from RUM
bytes.title: SvelteKit Production Monitoring â€” Edge Logs, Sentry in hooks.server.ts, and RUM Web Vitals
bytes.body:
SvelteKit gives you three distinct integration points for production observability, and each requires a different approach.

For server-side Sentry, `hooks.server.ts` is the right seam â€” wire `handleError` through `Sentry.handleErrorWithSentry()`. This captures errors from both SSR renders and `+server.ts` API routes with full request context via the `RequestEvent`.

Edge functions (routes exporting `export const config = { runtime: 'edge' }`) cannot use the Node.js Sentry SDK. Swap to `@sentry/cloudflare` or `@sentry/vercel-edge` depending on your adapter. `console.log` inside edge routes bypasses Node stdout entirely â€” output streams to your adapter's log drain (Vercel Log Drains, Cloudflare Logpush), so structure those logs as JSON for queryability.

Web Vitals via RUM require care: the `web-vitals` library only fires on hard navigations by default. In `+layout.svelte`, call your observers inside `afterNavigate` so LCP, CLS, and INP re-register after each client-side route transition. Tag each beacon with `page.route.id` from `$app/state` for per-route dashboards.

bytes.topic: Middleware â€” run at the edge before render, auth guards, A/B testing, geolocation, rate limiting
bytes.title: SvelteKit Hooks â€” Edge Middleware for Auth Guards, A/B Testing & Geolocation
bytes.body:
SvelteKit's `handle` hook in `src/hooks.server.ts` is your edge middleware layer â€” it intercepts every request before any `+page.server.ts` or `+server.ts` runs. Use it to enforce auth guards by inspecting cookies and redirecting unauthenticated users, inject A/B variant flags into `event.locals`, or gate routes by geolocation from request headers.

For auth, read the session cookie via `event.cookies.get()`, validate it, and either `redirect(303, '/login')` or populate `event.locals.user`. Downstream `load` functions and form actions then access `locals.user` without re-validating.

For A/B testing, hash `event.request.headers.get('x-forwarded-for')` or a stable cookie into a bucket, store the variant in `locals.variant`, and render different UI branches in your `+page.svelte`.

Rate limiting fits here too â€” maintain a `Map` keyed by IP in module scope (or use a KV store like Cloudflare's), check request count, and return a `new Response('Too Many Requests', { status: 429 })` before `resolve(event)` is ever called. Chain multiple concerns with `sequence()` from `@sveltejs/kit/hooks`.
